summary: Adversarial audit of the 3 named un-audited threeway residual surfaces (refstore remote push-CAS, backfill resumability, gitcas merge-tree determinism) + cutover composition; refute then worktree-repro-confirm each finding
logs: Find complete: 13 candidate findings (9 from finders + 4 main-read) -> refute
Refute complete: 5 survived (>=2/3 real+reachable), 8 refuted/uncertain -> confirm survivors

confirmed_reproduced.title: A single malformed event/index blob on the bus wedges EVERY append's idempotency scan AND every gate run (total-bus DoS) — refstore's raw reader has no well_formed guard, so the gate's drop-not-raise protection is bypassed upstream
confirmed_reproduced.line: 199 (_iter_local: `yield from_json_obj(json.loads(raw))`) + 195 (`entry = json.loads(idx); entry["path"]`); consumed at 116/138 (_find_verified_idempotent), 149 (id-collision scan), 211 (all_events)
confirmed_reproduced.repro_summary: CONFIRMED end-to-end against HEAD (73c285a2). A single malformed raw event blob (or malformed index entry) on the bus wedges RefEventStore._iter_local with an uncaught KeyError, which crashes EVERY append() (idempotency scan at refstore.py:116/138 and id-collision scan at 149 both iterate _iter_local) and EVERY reader (all_events/iter_events at 206/211) — a total-bus DoS. The trigger is the in-scope availability actor: an insider seat with bus push access crafts a raw event commit (no signature claimed — this is NOT forgery) whose event blob at events/<brief>/<id>.json omits a from_json_obj-required key (e.g. `payload`), plus its index entry. from_json_obj (envelope.py:166-178) dereferences obj["id"]/obj["payload"]/obj["from"]... with NO presence/type validation, so _iter_local raises KeyError at refstore.py:199 the instant it reaches the bad blob. A malformed INDEX entry missing `path` is a sibling vector (refstore.py:196, entry["path"]). Critically, gate.run_gate materializes store.all_events() at gate.py:168 BEFORE verify_and_reduce, and that call is OUTSIDE run_gate's try (which only begins at the step-3 "evaluate" block), so the KeyError escapes the gate for every candidate before the well_formed drop guard (gate.py:55 / envelope.well_formed) ever runs. The gate.py:164 comment "Bus EVENTS are already totality-guarded by well_formed" is false for this deserialization-time crash: well_formed only guards already-DESERIALIZED Events; the crash happens during deserialization, upstream of it. Verified the healthy baseline first (all_events returned 1 event), then a single injected blob crashed both paths. (Severity MAJOR not CRITICAL because raw injection requires bus push access and is recoverable by a manual ref rewrite/revert of the bad commit; but it is a one-shot wedge of the entire shared coordination plane with no in-band recovery, so it is a genuine availability defect, not a nit.)
confirmed_reproduced.evidence:
Repro mirrored the repo's own _inject_raw_event helper (tests/unit/test_threeway_refstore.py:71). pytest result: `2 passed in 0.80s` for tests/unit/_audit_threeway_refstore_py_1.py (both assert pytest.raises(KeyError), i.e. the code DOES crash as predicted). Direct-script run captured the exact uncaught tracebacks:
  before injection: all_events -> 1 events (healthy)
  injected one malformed (no-payload) raw event blob
  === all_events() after injection ===
  Traceback ... File ".../threeway/refstore.py", line 211, in all_events / line 199, in _iter_local: yield from_json_obj(json.loads(raw)) / File ".../threeway/envelope.py", line 171, in from_json_obj: payload=obj["payload"] -> KeyError: 'payload'
  === append(legit2) after injection ===
  Traceback ... File ".../threeway/refstore.py", line 138, in append / line 116 _find_verified_idempotent / line 199 _iter_local / envelope.py line 171 -> KeyError: 'payload'
Command: `env -u GIT_INDEX_FILE PYTHONPATH="$PWD" /Users/hyungkoookkim/Content/.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/_audit_threeway_refstore_py_1.py -q -p no:cacheprovider`. Worktree left clean (repro file deleted; git status empty).
confirmed_reproduced.fix_sketch: Move the well_formed totality guarantee UPSTREAM into the deserialization reader, so the reducer's drop-not-raise contract actually holds at the source. In RefEventStore._iter_local (refstore.py:184-199), wrap the per-entry deserialization in a try and SKIP (drop, never raise) any blob that fails to parse into a well-formed Event: parse the index entry defensively (e.g. `entry = json.loads(idx); path = entry.get("path"); if not isinstance(path, str): continue`), then `try: ev = from_json_obj(json.loads(raw)) except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError, json.JSONDecodeError): continue`, and additionally `if not well_formed(ev): continue` so a present-but-wrong-typed field is also dropped. This makes _iter_local total (a malformed blob is invisible, exactly as the gate already assumes), keeps a legitimate append's idempotency/id-collision scans and all readers functioning around the poison blob, and means the gate.py:164 comment becomes true. Alternatively (defense in depth), also harden from_json_obj to return None / raise a single typed MalformedEvent that _iter_local catches, rather than a bare KeyError. Add the two repro vectors (missing-payload event blob; missing-path index entry) as regression tests asserting all_events()/append() SUCCEED (skip the bad blob) after injection.

confirmed_reproduced.title: Non-atomic manifest write wedges BOTH the cutover's documented "retry resumes cheaply" path AND rollback — a crash/ENOSPC mid-write leaves a truncated manifest that backfill() and restore_from_manifest() both fail to json.loads with an unguarded JSONDecodeError
confirmed_reproduced.repro_summary: CONFIRMED. threeway/cursor_backfill.py writes its rollback/resume manifest non-atomically (line 88: `man.write_text(json.dumps(...))` — a truncating open('w')+write+close with NO tmp-file+atomic-rename), and BOTH readers do an unguarded `json.loads` (line 79 in backfill() under `if man.exists()`, line 105 in restore_from_manifest()). A crash / OOM / ENOSPC partway through the line-88 write leaves a truncated-but-existent manifest. The trigger is reachable and documented: run_cutover (cutover.py:168) calls cursor_backfill.backfill(coord_root) at step 5b, AFTER steps 3-4 have committed the events ref + 6 cursor refs and DELIBERATELY left them in place (cutover.py:162-167: "5b failure is recoverable, retry resumes cheaply, tearing down would discard all the successful appends"). With a truncated manifest, (a) the documented "retry resumes cheaply" path raises JSONDecodeError at line 79 and can NEVER resume, AND (b) restore_from_manifest() raises JSONDecodeError at line 105 so rollback is ALSO impossible. The cutover cannot be cleanly re-run either, since the steps-3/4 refs already exist and preflight_bus_init refuses (force=True is exactly the escape the no-teardown doctrine was built to avoid). The exact written invariant ("retry resumes cheaply") is violated. I lowered the guessed CRITICAL to MAJOR: the trigger requires a process death / ENOSPC precisely during the manifest write (a narrow window — the manifest is a few hundred bytes for 6 seats), and legacy sent/ remains authoritative so there is no silent data corruption or authority loss (operator-recoverable by hand-deleting the truncated manifest); it wedges both automated recovery paths but does not silently complete wrong.
confirmed_reproduced.fix_sketch: Two coordinated changes. (1) Make the manifest write atomic: in backfill(), write to a sibling temp file then os.replace() onto the final path — e.g. `tmp = man.with_suffix(man.suffix + ".tmp"); tmp.write_text(payload); os.replace(tmp, man)` — so the manifest is either fully present or absent, never truncated (os.replace is atomic within a filesystem). (2) Harden both readers against a corrupt/partial manifest from any other cause: in backfill() (line 79) and restore_from_manifest() (line 105), catch json.JSONDecodeError (and a missing/wrong-schema body) and treat a corrupt manifest as "not yet committed" — for backfill() that means falling through to the fresh-archive branch (so the retry re-derives original_iso from the seen/*.txt that step 5b has not yet rewritten — still ISO at first-resume) rather than wedging; for restore_from_manifest() raise a clear, typed recoverable error (or, paired with the atomic write, this case becomes unreachable). With (1) alone the truncation window is eliminated; (2) defends against partial writes from non-atomic-rename filesystems and makes the documented "retry resumes cheaply" invariant actually hold. Add a regression pin mirroring the repro (truncate manifest -> backfill() resumes, restore_from_manifest() does not raise).

confirmed_reproduced.id: threeway/gitcas.py — git plumbing for the byte-deterministic merge gate-2
confirmed_reproduced.title: merge-tree is NOT byte-deterministic across seats: _env pins commit identity but NOTHING pins the merge ALGORITHM, so ambient merge.renames / merge.renameLimit / diff config makes two seats disagree on mergeability and integration_sha
confirmed_reproduced.repro_summary: Confirmed against HEAD: gitcas.merge_tree (threeway/gitcas.py:56-63) pins NO merge-algorithm config, so two seats whose ambient git config differs in merge.renames return DIFFERENT (tree, clean) for IDENTICAL (base, branch) inputs — a byte-determinism break in the merge gate. Root cause: _DET_ENV (gitcas.py:13-21) pins only AUTHOR/COMMITTER identity+date and is applied ONLY to commit_tree/commit_on (gitcas.py:71,164). merge_tree routes through _run -> _env (gitcas.py:24-29), and _env strips ONLY GIT_INDEX_FILE (line 26) — it does NOT strip GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL/GIT_CONFIG_SYSTEM and passes NO `-c merge.renames`/`-c merge.renameLimit`/`-c diff.algorithm`. I built a repro that calls the real gitcas.merge_tree with a base file renamed+edited on one branch and edited in place on the other, injecting the host's merge.renames setting via GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL (the exact ambient channel _env fails to strip — not a test artifact). The impact is concrete in gate.py:201-203: the `clean` bool directly drives the verdict (clean -> recompute+commit_tree+CAS main; not clean -> REJECTED "merge not clean"). So a rename-aware seat MERGES while a rename-blind seat REJECTS the same candidate, and even when both call it clean their recomputed integration_sha trees differ (e22635f vs 47224f8). Honest reachability caveat (matches the finding): this needs the seats' ambient merge config to actually differ — environmental — but that config is per-host and unguarded, and merge.renameLimit silently disables rename detection above a file-count threshold, so a large-diff candidate can flip an individual seat with no config change at all. No harness-level pin masks it (grep of threeway/, tests/, conftest found no merge/diff config).
confirmed_reproduced.evidence:
Command: env -u GIT_INDEX_FILE PYTHONPATH="$PWD" /Users/hyungkoookkim/Content/.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/_audit_threeway_gitcas_py___git_plumbing_for_the_byte_deterministic_merge_gate_2.py -q -p no:cacheprovider -s
Key output (git 2.50.1), both calls into the REAL gitcas.merge_tree with the only difference being the ambient merge.renames in GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL:
  Seat A (merge.renames=true):  clean=True   tree=e22635f5e3fcb9b0f1227d217ceb5820539314af
  Seat B (merge.renames=false): clean=False  tree=47224f855a689100b92d115a5523f486f5e00235
  AssertionError: gitcas.merge_tree returned DIFFERENT (tree, clean) for the SAME (base, branch) under different ambient merge.renames -> two seats disagree on mergeability and integration_sha. SeatA=('e22635f5e3fcb9b0f1227d217ceb5820539314af', True) == SeatB=('47224f855a689100b92d115a5523f486f5e00235', False)
  1 failed in 0.23s
The repro asserts the determinism contract (same inputs -> same (tree, clean)); it FAILS on HEAD, demonstrating the bug. Repro file deleted afterward; `git status --porcelain` returned empty (worktree clean, nothing committed).
confirmed_reproduced.fix_sketch: Mirror _DET_ENV for the merge ALGORITHM, not just commit identity. In gitcas.merge_tree, pass the merge-algorithm config explicitly via `-c` flags on the merge-tree invocation so the result is host-independent: `git -c merge.renames=<pinned> -c merge.renameLimit=<pinned-or-0/large> -c diff.algorithm=<pinned> -C <repo> merge-tree --write-tree <base> <branch>`. Pin a single canonical policy across all seats (e.g. merge.renames=true with a fixed, generous merge.renameLimit so the renameLimit threshold can never silently flip an individual seat on a large diff, plus diff.algorithm=myers or histogram chosen once). Additionally harden _env (gitcas.py:24-29) to strip GIT_CONFIG_GLOBAL and GIT_CONFIG_SYSTEM (and GIT_CONFIG_COUNT/GIT_CONFIG_KEY_*/GIT_CONFIG_VALUE_*) so ambient host config can never leak into ANY plumbing call — the same belt-and-suspenders posture as the existing GIT_INDEX_FILE strip. Add a regression test asserting gitcas.merge_tree returns identical (tree, clean) under both merge.renames=true and =false ambient settings.

confirmed_reproduced.id: threeway/cutover.py run_cutover — the end-to-end composition of preflight + refstore append + teardown + cursor_backfill + advance_cursor-1
confirmed_reproduced.title: force=True re-run reads already-scalar seen/*.txt as ISO cursors in step 4 -> over-advances the cursor REF past unread events (ref-blob vs seen/*.txt diverge)
confirmed_reproduced.line: 150-157 (step 4: _read_iso_cursors at :153 -> iso_to_seq_map -> advance_cursor at :156); root in cursor_backfill.iso_to_seq_map at threeway/cursor_backfill.py:43-54; _read_iso_cursors at threeway/cutover.py:52-54
confirmed_reproduced.repro_summary: CONFIRMED. On a force=True re-run of run_cutover (the documented "acknowledge pre-existing refs/threeway/*" path), step 4 re-reads seen/<seat>.txt as ISO cursors — but after the first run's backfill() (cutover.py:168) those files hold SCALAR seqs, not ISO. The scalar is fed through cursor_backfill.iso_to_seq_map (cursor_backfill.py:43-54): iso_dash = iso[:11] + iso[11:].replace(":","-") turns scalar '3' into '3', then the per-event compare `ts <= iso_dash` is `'2026-06-17T..' <= '3'`, which is lexicographically TRUE for every event (any scalar with leading digit >= '3'). So the seat maps to N (the head), and advance_cursor (refstore.py:239, monotonic-forward) pushes the cursor REF from its correct seq up to N. The cursor ref then diverges from the seen/*.txt scalar and silently marks unread events as read (authority/read-state loss, not a crash). I built a tmp git repo with 5 events at distinct hourly timestamps and director's real ISO cursor at 17:00:00Z (covers seq 1-3). First run (force=False) correctly set cursor_seq('director')==3 and backfill rewrote seen/director.txt to '3'. The force=True re-run then over-advanced director's cursor REF 3 -> 5. I also confirmed iso_to_seq_map directly: real ISO '2026-06-17T17:00:00Z' -> {director:3}, but scalar '3' -> {director:5}. Severity MAJOR not CRITICAL: it requires the documented-but-uncommon force=True re-run after a completed/partial first run, the integrity/forgery class is unaffected, and only seats whose scalar seq has a leading digit >= '3' over-advance (leading digit '1'/'2' maps to 0 and is monotonic-blocked, though that path still clobbers seen.txt to 0 via backfill's no-manifest branch — a related read-state corruption).
confirmed_reproduced.evidence:
$ env -u GIT_INDEX_FILE PYTHONPATH="$PWD" /Users/hyungkoookkim/Content/.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/_audit_threeway_cutover_py_..._1.py -q -p no:cacheprovider
E       AssertionError: force re-run OVER-ADVANCED director cursor REF past unread events: true seq 3 -> 5 (events 4,5 silently marked read)
E       assert 5 == 3
tests/unit/_audit_..._1.py:85: AssertionError
1 failed in 2.70s
(The earlier in-test asserts passed: first-run cursor_seq('director')==3 and seen/director.txt=='3', so only the final post-rerun assertion at line 85 failed.)
Direct mechanism confirmation:
$ python -c "from threeway import cursor_backfill; names=[5 hourly event filenames]; print(cursor_backfill.iso_to_seq_map(names,{'director':'2026-06-17T17:00:00Z'})); print(cursor_backfill.iso_to_seq_map(names,{'director':'3'}))"
ISO 17:00 -> {'director': 3}
scalar 3  -> {'director': 5}
iso_dash for 3 = '3'
'2026-06-17T15-00-00Z' <= '3' ? True
confirmed_reproduced.fix_sketch: Make the cursor-source read in step 4 robust to already-migrated state, since force=True explicitly anticipates re-running over a prior cutover. Cleanest: when the cursor-backfill manifest already exists (cursor_backfill._manifest_path(coord_root)), step 4 must source each seat's authoritative seq from the manifest's archived "iso_to_seq" map (the same values backfill() re-applies) instead of re-deriving from seen/*.txt — which by then hold scalars. Equivalently, _read_iso_cursors / iso_to_seq_map should detect a non-ISO (purely-numeric) cursor value and either pass it through as the already-computed scalar seq or read the original ISO from the manifest's "original_iso" before mapping. A defensive belt-and-suspenders guard: have iso_to_seq_map reject a cursor string that doesn't match the ISO/filename-ts shape (raise CursorCorruptionError-style) rather than silently lexicographically comparing a bare integer against full timestamps, so the failure is loud rather than a silent over-advance. The advance_cursor monotonic-forward contract is correct and should stay; the bug is purely the wrong input seq fed to it on the re-run.

confirmed_reproduced.title: backfill() manifest write is non-atomic; a truncated manifest wedges the resume path
confirmed_reproduced.repro_summary: CONFIRMED. backfill() writes the cursor-backfill manifest non-atomically via man.write_text(json.dumps(...)) at threeway/cursor_backfill.py:88. A crash/kill/ENOSPC mid-write leaves a TRUNCATED (prefix) or zero-byte manifest on disk. On retry, the resume branch at cursor_backfill.py:78-79 sees man.exists()==True and runs `obj = json.loads(man.read_text())` with NO guard, raising json.JSONDecodeError. Every subsequent retry re-hits the same unguarded read, so backfill() can NEVER complete -> permanent wedge. At the cutover level this is unrecoverable: run_cutover calls backfill() at cutover.py:168 (step 5b) OUTSIDE any try/teardown block (AST-verified: backfill is NOT inside any Try in run_cutover), and the cutover.py:162-167 comment explicitly does NOT tear down here, relying on "a retry resumes cheaply" — which is exactly the assumption a truncated manifest violates. The bad outcome is bounded (no data loss / no reader harm: the authority-flip marker has not been committed, so legacy sent/ stays authoritative per the cutover.py:163-164 comment), but the migration cannot auto-recover and requires manual deletion of the corrupt manifest. I wrote tests/unit/_audit_main_M1_manifest_nonatomic.py (3 tests: truncated-manifest wedge, zero-byte wedge, clean-backfill control) — all 3 passed, confirming the JSONDecodeError on the truncated/empty resume path and that a clean backfill completes. Repro file deleted; worktree left clean (git status --porcelain empty).
confirmed_reproduced.evidence:
$ env -u GIT_INDEX_FILE PYTHONPATH="$PWD" /Users/hyungkoookkim/Content/.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/_audit_main_M1_manifest_nonatomic.py -q -p no:cacheprovider
  -> "3 passed in 0.02s" (the two wedge tests assert pytest.raises(json.JSONDecodeError) and pass; the control test confirms a clean backfill completes).

Direct call against HEAD (no pytest wrapper):
  manifest on disk (truncated): '{"schema": "cursor-backfill/1", "original_byt'
  --- calling backfill() on retry ---
  RAISED json.decoder.JSONDecodeError: Unterminated string starting at: line 1 column 33 (char 32)
  => backfill() WEDGED: resume branch (cursor_backfill.py:78-79) cannot recover

AST verification that step 5b is un-torn-down:
  $ ... ast walk of cutover.run_cutover -> "cursor_backfill.backfill() call is inside a try/except in run_cutover: False"

Source proof: cursor_backfill.py:78-79 `if man.exists(): obj = json.loads(man.read_text())` (no try); cursor_backfill.py:88 `man.write_text(json.dumps({...}))` (non-atomic); cutover.py:168 `cursor_backfill.backfill(coord_root)` (outside teardown), cutover.py:162-167 comment relies on cheap retry.
confirmed_reproduced.fix_sketch: Two-part fix in threeway/cursor_backfill.py. (1) Make the manifest write atomic in backfill() (cursor_backfill.py:87-95): write to a sibling temp file (e.g. man.parent / (man.name + ".tmp")) then os.replace(tmp, man) — atomic-rename guarantees readers see either the old absent state or the complete new manifest, never a torn prefix; this matches the same atomic-rename discipline used for cursor/ref writes elsewhere in the substrate. (2) Harden the resume branch (cursor_backfill.py:78-82) to tolerate a corrupt manifest left by a pre-atomicity crash: wrap json.loads(man.read_text()) in try/except (json.JSONDecodeError, also treat empty/whitespace text as absent) and, on parse failure, fall through to the fresh-archive path (treat the corrupt manifest as absent and re-derive). Re-deriving is safe here because the resume branch only runs when the seen/*.txt have NOT yet been rewritten to scalar in the partial run (a truncated manifest means the archive step never completed, so the originals are still ISO) — so re-reading them as "original" is correct, not a clobber. Validate the schema field after parse and treat a wrong/missing schema the same as corrupt. Add the truncated-manifest and zero-byte cases as regression tests alongside test_backfill_is_idempotent_preserves_original_anchor in tests/unit/test_threeway_cursor_backfill.py.

refuted.title: A corrupt (non-int) cursor blob permanently wedges a seat's cursor with no public repair/reset path — _read_cursor raises CursorCorruptionError on every read AND every advance

restore_from_manifest does NOT clear the resume anchor (the manifest), so any later backfill() takes the man.exists() resume branch and silently RE-APPLIES the scalar cursors — undoing the rollback and re-corrupting the legacy seen/*.txt

iso_to_seq_map's positional ISO->dash conversion mis-orders any cursor that is not exactly whole-second-Z form (fractional seconds, an explicit +00:00 offset, or a missing Z), assigning a seat a seq BELOW the events it already consumed -> after cutover that seat RE-PROCESSES already-seen events, and two hosts can disagree

refuted.id: threeway/gitcas.py — git plumbing for the byte-deterministic merge gate-1
refuted.title: merge_tree conflates a git ERROR (missing/bad object) with a real textual CONFLICT — gate misreports a mergeable candidate as 'merge not clean', driving a wrong ABORT/REWORK

refuted.id: threeway/gitcas.py — git plumbing for the byte-deterministic merge gate-3
refuted.title: build_tree_with leaves a stale scratch .idx in the repo dir if the process is killed mid-build (cosmetic litter — does not wedge future runs)
restore_from_manifest() does not clear the manifest; a later backfill() silently undoes the rollback
merge_tree conflates a git ERROR (rc 128) with a real CONFLICT (rc 1)
merge-tree result not pinned to be deterministic across git versions / rename detection

gaps.surface: threeway/cutover.py:54 _read_iso_cursors() and cursor_backfill.py:84-85 backfill() — both build their seat->ISO dict from `seen.iterdir()` (unordered) with the SEAT as the dict KEY
gaps.lens: DETERMINISM — but for DUPLICATE / case-colliding / non-canonical seat filenames, not the ISO-format mis-order that C3 already covers
gaps.why_unprobed: C3 attacked the ISO VALUE inside the seen file; nobody attacked the seen-FILENAME -> seat KEY mapping. `{p.stem: ... for p in seen.iterdir()}` silently last-write-wins if two files stem-collide on a case-insensitive FS (e.g. `Operator.txt` and `operator.txt`), or if a stray `operator.txt.bak`-style file with `.txt` suffix exists (`p.suffix=='.txt'` filters by suffix only, so `operator.foo.txt` -> stem `operator.foo`, a phantom non-seat key). iterdir order is FS-dependent, so two hosts can resolve the collision differently -> different cursor for the same seat after cutover. _SEATS in cutover.py:24 is a fixed 6-tuple, but `seq_map.get(seat, 0)` at cutover.py:155 silently falls back to 0 for any seat whose seen/*.txt is missing/misnamed — a seat that HAD consumed events gets cursor 0 and RE-PROCESSES the entire bus, with no error. No test plants a colliding or extra .txt; tests only use the canonical 6 names (test_threeway_cutover.py:115).

gaps.surface: threeway/refstore.py:255 advance_cursor() — its in-range validation reads `gitcas.list_index_seqs(self._repo, tip)` where tip = `self._sync()`
gaps.lens: AVAILABILITY/RESUMABILITY — a SPARSE/GAPPED index ref (missing index/<seq> blob) makes advance_cursor reject a perfectly valid cursor seq
gaps.why_unprobed: refstore-1 attacked a MALFORMED index blob wedging the idempotency SCAN; nobody attacked a MISSING-but-not-malformed index entry against advance_cursor's `seq not in valid` gate (refstore.py:257). After the cutover teardown path (or a partial CAS that landed the event blob but the index walk skips a `read_blob_at`-None entry, refstore.py:192-198 `if idx is None: continue`), `list_index_seqs` can return a set with a hole. advance_cursor(seat, N) where N is exactly the holed seq raises `ValueError(... beyond the event head)` even though N <= the true head — wedging that seat's cursor advance permanently with no repair path, AND the cutover step-4 loop (cutover.py:156) would raise -> teardown -> the whole cutover aborts on a recoverable index gap. The `if idx is None: continue` / `if raw is None: continue` swallow (refstore.py:192,197) is the silent-gap producer and is untested for downstream cursor impact.

gaps.surface: threeway/cutover.py:131-132 the append loop assigns seq via refstore.py:153 `max(seqs)+1` in carrier ITERATION order; legacy_projector.py:74 and cursor_backfill.py:40 each RE-SORT the same filenames with two SEPARATE regexes (_EVENT_NAME_RE vs _TS)
gaps.lens: DETERMINISM / CORRECTNESS — the seq an event GETS (projector order) vs the seq the cursor THINKS it has (iso_to_seq_map order) can diverge if the two orderings disagree
gaps.why_unprobed: The audit treated 'total order' as one thing, but it is computed THREE times by THREE code paths: (1) legacy_projector sorts by `(_EVENT_NAME_RE ts-group, full name)` to decide APPEND order -> which fixes each event's seq via max+1; (2) cursor_backfill.total_order sorts by `(_TS ts-token, full name)` to map ISO->seq; (3) the index/<seq:08d> numeric order. The two regexes differ: _EVENT_NAME_RE REQUIRES a valid from/to/kind grammar and SKIPS non-matching files (projector.py:70), while _TS only requires a leading ts token and total_order RAISES on a non-matching name (cursor_backfill.py:38). So a sent/ file that matches _TS but NOT _EVENT_NAME_RE (e.g. `2026-...Z-strayagent-to-x-foo.md`, a non-roster sender) is SKIPPED by the projector (never appended, no seq) yet COUNTED by total_order's index numbering -> every seat's iso_to_seq seq is shifted by +1 relative to the actual appended index seqs -> after cutover, cursors point one event too far / too short. Conversely total_order raises on it and aborts cutover step-4 -> teardown. Nobody constructed a filename in the _TS-yes/_EVENT_NAME_RE-no gap to prove the two orderings stay congruent.

gaps.surface: threeway/refstore.py:264 advance_cursor writes the cursor via `gitcas.write_blob` + `cas_create_or_update_ref` on a ref that DIRECTLY points at a blob — interacting with git GC / loose-object pruning and the cutover _snapshot_refs/_teardown of cursor refs (cutover.py:67,96)
gaps.lens: RESUMABILITY — a cursor blob OID captured in the pre-run snapshot can be GC'd / unreachable by teardown time, so the restore `update-ref ref <oid>` fails
gaps.why_unprobed: _teardown (cutover.py:96-97) does `update-ref ref oid` with check=True for any ref that existed pre-run. A cursor ref points at a BLOB that is referenced ONLY by that ref (no commit holds it). If, between _snapshot_refs and a mid-cutover failure, that cursor was advanced (new blob) and the OLD blob became unreferenced, an interleaved `git gc`/`git prune` (or `git repack`) on the shared repo can delete the snapshotted OID -> teardown's restore raises CalledProcessError -> TeardownError, leaving the cursor at the NEW (wrong) value and the events ref possibly half-restored. The whole cutover doc claims teardown 'restores EXACT prior state'; the GC/unreachable-blob interaction is unprobed (no test runs gc between snapshot and teardown), and shared-repo concurrent git ops are exactly the threat model (insider seat / benign concurrent writer).

gaps.surface: threeway/cursor_backfill.py:78-82 backfill()'s resume branch reads `json.loads(man.read_text())` then `obj['iso_to_seq']` — and restore_from_manifest reads `obj['original_bytes']` (cursor_backfill.py:112)
gaps.lens: RESUMABILITY — a SCHEMA-VALID but KEY-INCOMPLETE manifest (KeyError, not JSONDecodeError) from a future/older writer or a partial fsync of the json STRUCTURE
gaps.why_unprobed: M1/CB-1 attacked a TRUNCATED manifest -> JSONDecodeError. But the manifest is written `indent=2, sort_keys=True` (cursor_backfill.py:95), so a partial write can land VALID JSON missing a key (e.g. the dict closes early at a key boundary is impossible, but a manifest written by an older schema, OR hand-repaired, OR with `iso_to_seq` present but `original_bytes` absent) passes the schema check (restore only checks `obj.get('schema')`, cursor_backfill.py:106) yet raises bare `KeyError('original_bytes')` at line 112 / `KeyError('iso_to_seq')` at backfill line 80. restore_from_manifest validates SCHEMA but not the presence of `original_bytes`; backfill's resume branch validates NEITHER. So a manifest that survives the schema gate but lacks the key wedges BOTH paths with an unguarded KeyError — a distinct failure mode from the truncation that CB-1 reproduced, and it defeats the schema-check that was added as the guard.

gaps.surface: threeway/refstore.py:149 + 116-124 — the idempotency scan and the id-collision scan EACH call `self._iter_local()` (a full O(events) git walk) on EVERY append attempt; cutover.py appends ~768 carriers in a loop
gaps.lens: AVAILABILITY — second-order: a benign concurrent appender on the shared bus forces unbounded CAS retries, turning the documented 50-min O(n^2) cutover into a livelock that exhausts _max_attempts and aborts (-> full teardown of all prior appends)
gaps.why_unprobed: The two-process race test (test_threeway_refstore.py:226) proves no EVENT is lost under a race, and asserts `total_retries >= 1`. But it does NOT bound retries or test the cutover-scale interaction: each cutover append re-fetches + re-scans ALL prior events (refstore.py:137-149), and EACH CAS loss to a concurrent legitimate appender re-runs the full O(n) scan. With one steady concurrent writer during a 768-event cutover, append N can lose CAS repeatedly; if any single carrier exhausts _DEFAULT_MAX_ATTEMPTS=50 (refstore.py:42), append raises AppendContentionExceeded -> the cutover's except (cutover.py:134) tears down EVERY successful append. A single insider seat doing benign appends during the (expected, 50-min, no-progress) cutover window can therefore DoS the entire cutover with zero forged events. Nobody modeled sustained concurrent contention against the cutover composition specifically (the race test is 2 short processes, not a long cutover vs a steady writer).

gaps.surface: threeway/gitcas.py:61 merge_tree uses `p.stdout.splitlines()[0]` to extract the tree OID, and gate consumers feed it to commit_tree (gitcas.py:66) for integration_sha
gaps.lens: DETERMINISM / CORRECTNESS — merge-tree's OUTPUT FORMAT and the empty/whitespace-only-stdout edge, distinct from the rename-config nondeterminism of M4
gaps.why_unprobed: M4 attacked merge ALGORITHM config (renames/renameLimit). But the OUTPUT PARSING is also format-fragile and unprobed: `git merge-tree --write-tree` on a CONFLICT prints the tree OID on line 1 THEN a conflict section; on SUCCESS prints just the OID. The code takes `splitlines()[0]` and relies on returncode for clean (gitcas.py:62-63). However, if a future/older git or a config like `merge-tree` informational messages on stdout shifts line 1, or stdout is empty on a degenerate input (returncode 0 but no tree printed, e.g. identical trees in some git versions print nothing), `tree` becomes None WITH clean=True (gitcas.py:62 guards empty stdout -> None, but returncode 0 -> clean=True) -> a (None, True) result that a caller may treat as 'mergeable, integration tree = None' and pass None into commit_tree -> git error or a wrong integration_sha. M3 covered rc-128 vs rc-1; nobody covered rc-0-with-no-tree or the line-1-not-OID format assumption across git versions, which is the same cross-version determinism threat as M4 but on the parse side.

critic.overall: The 13 triaged candidates thoroughly cover the FIRST-ORDER versions of each surface (one malformed blob, one non-atomic write, one rename-config divergence) but systematically miss SECOND-ORDER and INTERACTION lenses on the same files. The largest unprobed cluster is ORDERING CONGRUENCE: 'total order' is computed by three independent code paths (legacy_projector._EVENT_NAME_RE-sorted append order which fixes seq via max+1; cursor_backfill._TS-sorted iso_to_seq numbering; the numeric index) and nobody proved they stay congruent when a filename falls in the gap between the two regexes — that is a determinism/correctness hole strictly distinct from C3 (which only attacked the ISO VALUE, not the filename->seat KEY or the projector-vs-backfill ordering). The second cluster is the seen/*.txt -> seat KEY mapping (`{p.stem: ...}` over unordered iterdir with suffix-only filtering): case/stem collisions and stray .txt files silently last-write-wins or inject phantom keys, and the `seq_map.get(seat, 0)` fallback (cutover.py:155) silently resets a real seat's cursor to 0 -> full bus reprocess, with no error and no test. The third gap is RESUMABILITY of the teardown's cursor-ref restore against git GC of now-unreachable blob OIDs — the cutover doc claims 'restores EXACT prior state' but the snapshotted blob can be pruned by a concurrent git op. Two more real gaps: a schema-VALID-but-key-MISSING manifest defeats the schema guard with a bare KeyError in both backfill resume and restore (distinct from M1's truncation -> JSONDecodeError); and a steady benign concurrent appender can livelock the 50-min O(n^2) cutover into AppendContentionExceeded -> total teardown (an availability DoS with zero forged events, never modeled — the race test is two short processes, not a long cutover vs a sustained writer). All six are reachable against HEAD with concrete triggers; the highest-value ones to reproduce first are the ordering-congruence gap and the seen-filename->seat-key gap, since both silently corrupt cursors after a successful-looking cutover rather than failing loudly.
